title: Making a leather glasses case
subtitle: Hand-stitch a slim hard-shell glasses case from vegetable-tanned leather over a chipboard inner former
excerpt: How to make a leather glasses case. Covers cutting the leather cover panels, gluing and stitching around a rigid chipboard inner, and finishing the edges and a press-stud closure.
sourceNotes: Traditional leatherwork casing technique. Al Stohlman, The Art of Hand Sewing Leather (referenced principles, not reproduced). UK leatherworking trade practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of thinning leather by shaving the flesh side at a shallow angle with a sharp knife or dedicated skiver tool. Used to reduce the thickness of edges, folds, and overlaps so they do not create a visible ridge or bulk when glued or stitched. Edges that will be folded over a former are skived back 10 to 15 mm from the edge to allow a clean, flat turn-in. Effective skiving produces a wedge-shaped cross-section that tapers to near-zero at the edge.

content.text: A leather glasses case over a rigid card inner holds its shape and keeps the lenses safe. Cut the leather panels to the former size,
the edges thin, glue the leather around the card former, and saddle-stitch the two halves together at the spine.

Cut two pieces of 3 mm grey board or chipboard to the glasses dimensions: typically 160 mm × 70 mm, or traced from the glasses frame. These are the lid and base. Round the corners with a punch or scissors. Check the glasses fit easily between the two pieces with 5 to 8 mm clearance all round.

From 1.2 mm veg-tan, cut two cover panels each 25 mm larger on all sides than the former. This gives a 12 mm fold-over allowance. Skive the 12 mm edge allowance on the flesh side of each panel so the edge tapers to around 0.5 mm. The skived allowance folds cleanly over the former edge without creating a ridge on the face of the case.
Glue the leather to the former

Apply contact adhesive to both the flesh side of the leather and the face of the former. Allow to become touch-dry. Press the leather onto the former, face-side up, centred. Fold the edge allowance over the back of the former, working opposite sides alternately (front and back, then left and right. At corners, cut a small V into the allowance and fold neatly. Press firmly all around. Repeat for the second panel.
Join the halves and fit the closure

Punch stitch holes along one long edge of each covered panel) the spine side. Saddle-stitch the two panels together at the spine with waxed linen thread. Burnish all exterior edges with water and the burnisher. Set a press-stud on the front edge of the lid and the corresponding base position: the case closes with a clean snap and the stud barely shows on the leather face.
